frameworks.name: causal connectivity / functional circuit modulation
frameworks.one_line: Effective synaptic connectivity measured causally and treated as task-phase dynamic.

frameworks.name: perceptual decision-making (evidence accumulation, commitment, motor readout)
frameworks.one_line: Sequential-phase decision computations frame the behavioral epochs probed.

frameworks.name: fixed-anatomy / flexible-function neural circuit
frameworks.one_line: Extends fixed-wiring view with fast connectivity modulation as a mechanism.

core_question: Does the causal connectivity among cortical excitatory neurons change across different phases of a decision-making task?
key_assumptions: All-optical perturbation reveals causal, not merely correlational, connectivity
Anatomical synaptic connectivity is approximately fixed on task timescales
Decision-making can be decomposed into distinct sequential epochs
Layer 2/3 retrosplenial cortex participates in navigation-based decisions
manipulations: Behavioral epoch (cue/decision vs later task phases vs no-task)
Single-cell optogenetic perturbations of L2/3 excitatory neurons
Task presence vs absence (in-task vs no-task baseline)
key_findings: In-task causal connectivity differed from no-task causal connectivity
Connectivity differences were selective to the cue / decision phase
These differences tapered off in later stages of the task
interpretations: Fast modulation of connectivity is a prevalent mechanism in neural circuit function
Different task phases are implemented partly by phase-specific reweighting of connectivity
